message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In current severe global emergency situation of 2019-nCov outbreak, it is imperative to identify vulnerable and susceptible groups for effective protection and care. Recently, studies found that 2019-nCov and SARS-nCov share the same receptor, ACE2. In this study, we analyzed five large-scale bulk transcriptomic datasets of normal lung tissue and two single-cell transcriptomic datasets to investigate the disparities related to race, age, gender and smoking status in
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  gene expression and its distribution among cell types. We didn’t find significant disparities in
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  gene expression between racial groups (Asian vs Caucasian), age groups (>60 vs <60) or gender groups (male vs female). However, we observed significantly higher
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  gene expression in former smoker’s lung compared to non-smoker’s lung. Also, we found higher
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  gene expression in Asian current smokers compared to non-smokers but not in Caucasian current smokers, which may indicate an existence of gene-smoking interaction. In addition, we found that
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  gene is expressed in specific cell types related to smoking history and location. In bronchial epithelium,
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  is actively expressed in goblet cells of current smokers and club cells of non-smokers. In alveoli,
                  <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                  is actively expressed in remodelled AT2 cells of former smokers. Together, this study indicates that smokers especially former smokers may be more susceptible to 2019-nCov and have infection paths different with non-smokers. Thus, smoking history may provide valuable information in identifying susceptible population and standardizing treatment regimen.
                </jats:p>
title: Bulk and single-cell transcriptomics identify tobacco-use disparity in lung gene expression of ACE2, the receptor of 2019-nCov
